attributes.stem:
A 32-year-old woman presents to the antenatal clinic at 34 weeks gestation for routine follow-up. This is her second pregnancy. Her first pregnancy three years ago resulted in a forceps-assisted vaginal delivery complicated by a third-degree perineal tear (3C tear) at 37 weeks + 2 days gestation. She describes this birth as traumatic and is very concerned about the prospect of another vaginal birth. She has been experiencing some mild faecal incontinence since her previous delivery. Her current pregnancy has been uncomplicated, with normal antenatal investigations. The estimated fetal weight is on the 75th percentile.

She has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. On examination, she appears well. Her pulse rate is 72/min, blood pressure 120/80 mmHg, respiratory rate 16/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 36.7°C. The fetus is in a cephalic presentation with good fetal movements felt.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate plan for delivery?
options: Book and counsel for an elective caesarean section at 39 weeks
Book and counsel for an elective caesarean section at 38 weeks
Planned vaginal delivery with early epidural
Book and counsel for an elective caesarean section at 37 weeks
attributes.explanation:
This patient has a history of a third-degree perineal tear (obstetric anal sphincter injury, OASI) from her previous forceps-assisted delivery and is currently experiencing mild faecal incontinence. These factors significantly increase her risk of recurrent OASI with another vaginal delivery.

Book and counsel for an elective caesarean section at 39 weeks is the most appropriate plan. This timing allows for optimal fetal lung maturity while addressing the high risk of recurrent sphincter injury. The patient's current symptoms of faecal incontinence and previous traumatic experience also support this approach.

Book and counsel for an elective caesarean section at 38 weeks is not ideal as 39 weeks is the recommended gestational age for elective caesarean sections in uncomplicated pregnancies to optimise fetal lung maturity and reduce neonatal respiratory morbidity.

Planned vaginal delivery with early epidural would not be recommended as the primary option due to the high risk of recurrent OASI. The risk of recurrence is estimated to be 5-7 times higher in women with a previous OASI compared to those without.

Induction of labour at 39 weeks does not address the primary concern of recurrent OASI and may increase the risk of instrumental delivery, which could further increase the risk of severe perineal trauma.

Book and counsel for an elective caesarean section at 37 weeks is too early for an elective procedure in an otherwise uncomplicated pregnancy and may lead to unnecessary neonatal respiratory complications.

The final decision should be made after thorough counselling with the patient, discussing the risks and benefits of both vaginal delivery and caesarean section, taking into account her preferences, current symptoms, and plans for future pregnancies. If spontaneous labour occurs before the scheduled booked elective procedure the Caesarean section will be performed as an emergency Caesarean section.
learningPoints: Women with a previous obstetric anal sphincter injury (OASI) have an increased risk of recurrence in subsequent vaginal deliveries.
Elective caesarean section is offered as an option for delivery for women with a previous 3rd or 4th degree perineal tear to reduce the risk of recurrent OASI.
The decision for mode of delivery should be made after thorough counselling, considering the woman's preferences and individual risk factors.
Elective Caesarean sections should be booked from 39 weeks + to reduced the risk of neonatal respiratory complications
references: Royal College of Obstetricians and Gynaecologists. The Management of Third- and Fourth-Degree Perineal Tears (Green-top Guideline No. 29) [Internet]. 3rd ed. London: RCOG; 2015. Available from: https://www.rcog.org.uk/media/5jeb5hzu/gtg-29.pdf
